[Shawn] Hey, it’s André and Shawn. We are doing a soap opera series review today for the Art of Email Workshop. This one was submitted by Julie. We’ll provide a link, so you can see a version of this document as well. Julie’s just sat down butt in chair, and she’s written 26 pages, which is extraordinary. So first of all, congratulations to Julie for really doing the work. Second of all, though, we’re not going to review 26 pages. We wanna be clear. That’s not going to be helpful to anybody, and we would end up jamming a knife in our eyes if we had to do that. So what we’re going to do instead is we’re just gonna jump around a little bit. We’re going to give more of a high level, I say this like we have any idea. We have no idea what we’re going to do. Something’s about to happen, I guess, is the best way to think about this. So I’m gonna get started, I’ll hand it off to André, and we’re gonna ping pong back and forth. And then at some point, one of us is gonna say no loss and pass out, and then we’re gonna click stop record. All right. So a couple of things I notice immediately, and just by way of context, I’ve looked at this a little bit, but I haven’t read all 26 pages, and there’s a reason for that. I always wanna have the reader’s first experience when I look, and when I do a review, as much as possible. I don’t want to have read something, and then thought about it, and then come back to it, and give feedback on it, ’cause that’s a very different feedback than if you just read something. ‘Cause most people who get your emails, the initial reaction is their reaction. So that’s kinda what I’m trying to replicate here. All right. So here are a few things that are sort of high level. One, right in the beginning, Julie says, my product is a signature 12 week course aimed at women 40 to reclaim and increase their physical strength, et cetera, et cetera. This is really important, because as we’ve said in the past, all roads lead to Rome, right? So there is a product somewhere in the future. There’s something that this is all about. This soap opera series doesn’t lead directly to the product. That’s not what a soap opera series does, but it certainly sets up the context for it. It does two things, essentially. One, it pulls people forward who are going to be a good fit for that course, at some point in the future. And it discourages people, subtly, over time, who aren’t. And both of those responsibilities are equally valuable. And knowing that sort of future destination makes that filtering in and out much easier. So to say this right up front, this is my product, this is what I’m doing, very important. I love how Julie has broken it into three parts. Act one, act two, and act three. Just as a thinking tool, I think that’s very helpful. I do this a lot, I write notes to myself, especially if I’m thinking about something over time, and I’m not writing it immediately, when I’m thinking about it, I’m constructing it, I like to put some notes in. Often I do that with pencil and paper, not on the screen itself. And you’ll also see that Julie has things in red here where like she’s giving all caps value. The magic happens outside, go outside, where to play and move, who gets to say how or where we move? I like this notes to herself kind of thing. Like, this is this reminder. This is what this thing is about. Right? And, it’s so easy to forget what the thing you’re writing is about. And we all do it. Especially if we’re writing about one thing, and then something interesting appears in our mind, and we start pulling on a thread, and the next thing you know, we look up and we realize we’re three miles down a side road, and we forgot what the thing we were writing was supposed to be about. It’s not always that explicit. Sometimes we don’t know we’ve done that until later, when we get feedback, and realize that we’ve done that. So just my initial overview of this is I love structure, and I love how Julie has integrated some structure with the three acts. I love how she has made some notes to herself to set the stage. And just in this first one, I love the subject line. The Magic Happens Outside. It makes me wanna read it. Like, what magic happens outside? Is my first thought. And right before I handed it off too, I love, this is raw. You can see it was just written, it’s not spaced well on everything yet, that all happens later. But I love this first line though, too, get down from there. Like, we’ve all had that experience. As soon as I read that, I was rocketed back to being a kid and having my mom, or my dad, or a grandparent or something like telling me to get the hell out of a tree, or something, when I was a kid. So we immediately know what that means. So, all right, handing it off to you, André, with all of that as intro for you to say something smart.

[André] Is it worth us admitting that to embody this audience of women 40 plus the two of us are wearing dresses?

[Shawn] I don’t know that we need to tell everybody everything. I mean, I think we should keep a little mystery. Well, I think he just made it clear to everybody why this is audio only.

[André] Oh, man. So it’s just me wearing a dress then, okay.

[Shawn] I don’t wanna point fingers. This is a partnership of equals. What choices you make, you do you, I’ll do me. If you’re wearing a dress, it’s all good. It’s all good, man.

[André] Yeah. So I think I’m just gonna reiterate a few things that Shawn’s just said, is that this document is a great example of what a shitty first draft is meant to look like. It is messy. There’s internal dialogue. I normally, if I’m in a Google Doc, I’m using the little comments section, and I’m making comments to myself. So, you know, this is all spaced weirdly, and the spelling’s all out, but that doesn’t matter, because when you start to read this thing, it really sucks you in. So I think this is, the opening few emails that I’ve read of this do an amazing job of creating this narrative that’s an interesting journey that people wanna be, well, the 40 plus women that are reading this. I can see somebody wanting to read the emails. And it’s really easy to read these sentences. They’re very short. They’re not much more than a line each. And it just pulls you along really nicely. At no point did I feel like this is hard work, reading this stuff. And, Julie’s got some nice little stories she starts to tee up inside of you, if we scroll down a bit.

[Shawn] Yeah, and before we scroll down, I’ll comment on a couple of things here, too. Just thinking out loud stuff, I say this all the time, read your stuff out loud, and to André’s point, yes, this is a shitty first draft, so keep that in mind, but read your stuff out loud, and then make the spacing, and make everything beautiful to the ear. And this is an observation I think I’ve heard from Robert Fritz. Yes, it was Robert Fritz. He said, we often write for the eye. Like, how does it look on the page? But most people are reading, I know this is true for me, are hearing our voice in our head, reading it out loud. You’re not hearing the sound, but you’re hearing a voice in your head reading. So don’t just write for the eye, write for the ear. But the thing that jumped out at me as I as I read these first few sentences is, listen to this out loud. I was too lazy and motivated to join a club, do sport again, or buy any equipment. And no, you wouldn’t find me in a gym. I felt pretty average. That’s how it’s written. But certainly, when you get to, I felt pretty average, there’s a pause, right? Like, it’s, I felt pretty dot, dot, dot average. Because that’s how it sounds, right? You would pause there, ’cause you could imagine Julie reflect, she’s kind of giving you the background. I was too lazy, I was unmotivated, I wasn’t gonna join a club, wasn’t going to be involved in athletics, not gonna buy equipment. There’s no way in hell you’re gonna find me in a gym. That’s like the setup. And the resolution is, I felt pretty average. That’s how it sounds. So you wanna write it that way. And then just one other part, I just happened to know from conversations with Julie, that Julie’s British. Julie’s British, but she lives in the US. There are some indicators in here that it’s British English. Just be aware of that, if you’re writing to an American audience, we don’t do sport in the United States, athletics. No one has a clue who the hell Mr. Blobby is. I’m gonna assume that’s a British thing, right? It’s subtle, but the way I think about it, it creates drag. Just a little bit of drag, right? Like, I wanna go through this, and I just want one word, one sentence to flow into the next. And anytime something slows down the flow, to me, it feels like drag. So when I read this sentence, I was too lazy and unmotivated to join a club, do sport again, I’m like, what? The hell you mean, do sport again? In the US, you do sports, right? There are sports, or athletics. But you don’t do sport, in the US. And again, I have no clue who the hell Mr. Blobby is, you know? I mean, I can imagine, the name kinda says it all. But, when I see a soft mint cartoon. What the hell’s a mint cartoon? Like, again, the moment I have to stop and ask myself questions, I’m pulled out of the narrative. And yes, I’m batshit crazy when I read, and I’m not a 40 plus woman, and no, I’m not wearing a dress. No judgment for André wearing his. But these are just these little details of just to think about the nuances, because this is good writing. To be clear, we don’t have to mess with the writing. This is good writing, these are good stories. So we’re going to focus on more of the nuances, because that’s where there is the focus. Okay. So-

[André] Yeah, if you go up slightly, well, I mean, we just read that part. But I think sometimes sentences come out that are just so good. Not just because they sound good in the context of reading a certain sentence, but there’s an implication of what it means when somebody, which is your prospect, let’s say, because they’re not a customer of said product, that it’s answering a voice in their head. And the one that she wrote here is, I felt pretty average. I think that’s such a great sentence. Just four words. And I think that must ring bells in her audience’s head, because, I doubt they’re all feeling like athletes, otherwise they wouldn’t be reading this thing, right? So it’s just like, very early on, in the very first few sentences, she’s entering their head, and said something that I’m sure would be resonating. Also, the point about Mr. Blobby, ’cause I know that this first part, she’s in London, so she’s talking about her experience in Southwest England, when she moved down to London. So maybe the reference to Mr. Blobby, maybe her audience is on both sides of the pond. I suppose she could rewrite that in a way that, I didn’t look like Mr. Blobby, and draw attention to the fact that it’s the American version of, insert American Mr. Blobby, whatever that is, ’cause I wouldn’t know. I know what Mr. Blobby is. But good in a way that doesn’t break Shawn’s level of friction. So it just feels like she’s making a quick pause to define what it is really quickly, without stopping people’s. And then it’s like, oh, okay, yes, I know what the American version is, therefore I know what Mr. Blobby is, or vice versa. But if her audience isn’t British at all, like there’s no British people that are customers, then yeah, then maybe just substitute Mr. Blobby for whatever your version is. You must have a Mr. Blobby.

[Shawn] Good thing we’re not doing a 26 page review around these paragraphs. And I don’t wanna dwell on this one, but there’s just so much nuance in all this too. And the other thing I would ask yourself is, is that the thing that’s in your prospect’s mind, right? And do they look in the mirror and think, ew, I look like a cartoon character. And are they aspiring to be a lean, mean, fighting machine? Again, not a 40 plus year old woman, but my guess is that language probably isn’t, just thinking out loud, I’m guessing that those aren’t the two distinctions that somebody is making. That there is a different pairing. There’s a different contrast that you can use here between, they don’t look like X, and they also don’t look like a fitness model, or something like that. Victoria’s Secret model, if you’re in the US. There’s some A and B contrast there that I think is probably more in your prospect’s head than the cartoon character versus the lean, mean, fighting machine. I think, I don’t know. All right, let’s eliminate Mr. Blobby, so we don’t end up comparing both sides of the Atlantic over and over again here. Although, we’re going to keep getting back into it, I can tell. Where do we wanna go next?

[André] I love it when there’s lots of short sentences in and amongst longer ones. And that contrast between long and short, when you’re reading it, it does pull you along. And Julie seems to have done it quite well. And, like she’s done here, don’t be afraid to start sentences with and. And, they always look really good. They don’t teach it at school.

[Shawn] Write like you talk, right? Exactly. People get so formal, right? And here’s a perfect example, right? She said, this is like the world’s greatest sentence. The black snot in my nose at the end of the day, after traveling on the tube. Again, that’s a British thing, we don’t have a tube in the United States. The black snot in my nose at the end of the day, after traveling on the tube was a constant reminder of the environmental stressors and constraints. Nobody talks like that, right? But the rest, there’s such a great conversational tone in here. You’re saying nope, you start sentences with and. Don’t get all formal, and talk about environmental stressors and constraints, right? No. The black snot at the end of your day after traveling on the tube was a constant reminder of London’s dirty air. That’s it. No one talks about environmental stressors and constraints. So if you’re going to use that language, go all in. Talk conversationally. There’s no dialogue in here, but especially in dialogue. And don’t say tube, we get it. I mean, maybe. I mean, I like what André said earlier about like, she’s mentioned that she’s from England, she’s talking about England. So it makes sense to talk about the tube. But, paranthetically, maybe, I would mention that’s the, whatever to, AKA blank in America. And even in America, we don’t have a single name for it. If you’re from Boston, I went to graduate school in Boston. If you’re in Boston, it’s the T. I lived in DC too, in DC it’s the metro. Other places have different names. Subway in New York City. So there’s no American equivalent of the tube. So, what do you do with this? Yeah, I don’t know how you solve that. I just introduced a problem I have no idea how to solve, so good luck, Julie, thank you. Nice to talk to you today, André, see ya.

[Shawn] Yeah, I don’t know. I’m just thinking out loud here, maybe there would be a way, but it’d have to be very top of the email. It could be in brackets saying, come with me on a journey to London. And it’s just letting people know that the context is gonna be somewhere else. And therefore, she’s allowed to go off script. And unless her audience are all British, even though Julie’s now living in the States, I don’t know. We should have found out about that, maybe, perhaps.

[Shawn] Then we wouldn’t get to rattle around as much. There’s something really cool here, and maybe it’s just the way the screen’s displaying too, but I love this. Wanna draw attention to it, which is the italicized sentence at the end, summarizing the idea. This rings so true for me, and I know this is probably my own crazy. But when I see this, I think, this is mastery stuff right here, which is, you’re saying all these things. You know, London was intellectually and culturally stimulating, a place to move, play and get stronger, nope, that wasn’t on my radar. You’ve got black snot, and you’ve got marginal creative, square pegs in round holes, and you got all this stuff. But then there’s this one sentence. Movement can happen everywhere. It’s like, it’s taking like all this stuff, and it’s just this beautiful pause. Today, I’m gonna tell you these 10 things, but then after those 10 things, I’m going to summarize what those 10 things mean in a simple sentence, four words. Movement can happen everywhere. Like damn. You can imagine somebody reading this, and if I’m thinking to myself, what the hell was this about? I just need to highlight this, click highlight, that’s what it’s about. I absolutely love that. And it’s a way that many people write, I’m guessing subconsciously. I know when I catch myself doing this well, it’s because my brain wants me to do it, because my brain is asking me to summarize things. It’s like I said this, I said that, I’ve said this, and then I kinda make the point, right? It’s just a thing for the reader. I absolutely love that. I don’t know your thoughts on that. I’ll leave it up here. Where do we wanna go next here? All right, I see we’ve got a list. Let’s talk about this list, maybe. Let’s see. I wasn’t an athlete, you already said that, so be careful. I wasn’t an athlete, I wasn’t planning a change of career. I was 20 years old, I didn’t know anyone who I’d seen do it, and I missed nature. That’s interesting, that’s incongruent. I like that, ’cause now, just reading this and that, these two things together, that’s a weird pairing. A weird pairing forces me to stop and say, what? Sometimes that’s a problem, but I think in this case it works. ‘Cause think about how these things go together. Like, what’s the progression here? Julie wasn’t an athlete. Okay. She’s not planning a change of career. All right. Is 20 years older than everyone who she’d seen jumping off the walls here. And I miss nature. That last one is, it’s different enough that I’m like, huh, I don’t know, it just caught my attention for some reason.

[André] Yeah. I’m in two minds about the bullets as well. I just think, potentially on a small mobile screen, where it isn’t as wide as what we’re looking at it now, that menu of stuff, one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight bullets crammed up onto a little mobile screen can look like a lot of text. Whereas, unless there’s a very strong case to have a bullets where you just wanna summarize something very quickly, so it kinda pops out. But I haven’t read anything inside there that couldn’t be on its own sentence, continuing down this this narrative. Pulling somebody towards the close, at high speed, perhaps. But it’s just that the bullet kind of slows everything down for me. And I think it’ll be even more jarring on a small screen. I don’t know, that’s just me thinking in the moment.

[Shawn] Yeah, when you said that, I was thinking eight bullets is five too many. That was the the first thought that popped into my head when I saw that. I think there’s too much here. I think I would just get rid of them entirely. Because, what’s the best way to think about this? There should be a staircase of tension being resolved, or sorry, tension being created in this email. And it’s ever rising. Yes, you can have a little undulation, but in general, the staircase wants to rise, step, by step, by step, through the entirety of this series. Now, because we open and close loops along the way, there is some undulation of that tension, which would show up in an oscilloscope, actually, André.

– That’s right.

– My turn to throw a oscilloscope in, right? But really, if we were to look at it on a graph over time, and so let’s just say, we have an XY graph, the horizontal line is time, the vertical line is tension, from beginning to end, we want there to be an ever rising tension. And this bulleted list just tears the tension out of this. It doesn’t set up tension. It tells me things that don’t create tension. And there are a lot of them together, which tends to repulse my attention a little bit. Like these are all things that should be told to me over the remainder of the series. Don’t tell them everything at once, ’cause now, there’s no mystery. I know too much now. I kinda know what’s coming. Like here’s a simple sense, 10 minutes of movement is enough to change how you feel and improve cognitive performance. There’s no mystery in that, you’ve told me everything there is about it. I’m like, okay, cool, I’ll just go for a 10 minute walk every day, and I’ll feel better. I’ll improve my cognitive, like there’s no more there, there’s nothing opened up for it. So I think I would get rid of all of these. The other thing too, just jump on before I hand it back to you. From my perspective, this is not an open loop to get me to read tomorrow. Did I choose a career change to embrace movement when I was busy being an independent filmmaker? More on that tomorrow. Well, obviously you did, that’s what the email is talking about. Like if you didn’t, this is gonna be a really weird soap opera series, because everything is about movement, and about your trip to Southern California. If I don’t already know that you’ve changed careers to embrace movement, I’m not paying attention to anything. There’s no open loop there. And at the end of the day, this sounds like I’m being a jerk, and I don’t mean this to sound like a jerk, I as the reader, I don’t really give a shit what you did, right? I mean, yes, I do. I like the story. But what I really want to know, if you really wanna open a loop that grabs my attention, is what is something about me, that’s valuable about me, that I’m going to find out tomorrow? That’s what I really want to know. So, this is not what I would use, but this open loop at the end, first of all, it should be the culmination of all the tension in the email itself. The email should be leading to this, this is not really an open loop, it’s a cliffhanger, right? So all of the tension in this email should lead up to this question that is a cliffhanger until tomorrow. And that question needs to be something that is of interest to the reader. Not just about the writer, I think I how I would describe that, and I don’t have a good idea for what that might be, but it would be something more along the lines of, what did I learn about X that you can use to improve Y? That’s not a great model, but it’s something like that. Or what did I learn that I never expected, and why is that important? And why have I realized why that’s so important? I mean, again, the wordsmithing isn’t important here, it’s the structure, it’s the idea. Funnel all the tension in this, like gradually ascend the tension in this entire, especially because it’s the first one. Set the stage, build the tension, focus the attention on a cliffhanger that all I wanna know is what the hell happens tomorrow. As soon as tomorrow’s email comes in, I already have this question in my mind, and let me jump into it. So anything to add to that before we move on to the next one?

[André] Yeah. I think subtext. So the whole idea of having a subtext, its power is in not drawing attention to it. It’s for people to feel, and come to that conclusion themselves. And allowing that to help them create this connection between the tension of A and B. So, one of the subtexts may be this thing. Ah, you know, she clearly used to be an independent filmmaker, now she’s doing this other thing. You don’t have to say much more than that, I mean, you don’t have to draw attention to that explicitly. It’s there, in the narrative. And I think that will pull more people forward. And then the other thing, there’s a few things that I liked in that last paragraph just below the bullets, that’s kind of hidden in that chunk of text there. And she’s written a few words that I just think are so cool. So, you know, being a weirdo, I kinda like the word weirdo.

[Shawn] ‘Cause you’re wearing a dress, what the hell? Of course.

[André] Well, that too. And then, here we go. More brave. So, I learned and leveraged to be stronger, more brave. I think that more brave part, that’s something, right? I mean, I think that’s a powerful word for people to hear, because they are probably certainly thinking it in their head. To make friends with fear, that’s also interesting. And then to become a happy mover, aging with confidence. I don’t know, there’s all these little pieces that I think are really nice. They just shouldn’t be all crammed up inside there.

[Shawn] Yeah, it’s funny, I think I would write that different, again, is it lyrical? So more brave to me sticks out, ’cause I think it should be braver. I mean, talk about really being the nuances of writing, but. It says, you probably have no interest in walking on a rail, or being a weirdo in the park, and that’s cool, but the principles I learned and leveraged to be stronger, braver, less fearful. Yeah, I think this could be smoother. I don’t know. Or not. I mean, yeah, I get hung up where it’s, just, I love the lyrical nature of writing. So the principles I learned and leveraged to be stronger, braver, less fearful. Yeah, the last thing doesn’t fit. ‘Cause you can hear it, right? If I say it, if I’m like, the principles that I learned and leveraged to be stronger, braver, make friends with fear, and become a happy mover, aging with, and now I’m like, what the heck? To me, this is two things. And I think this is what you’re feeling, too. This paragraph is actually two sentences. One is, you probably have no interest in walking on a rail, or being a weirdo in the park, and that’s cool, period. But, comma, the principles I learned and leveraged to be stronger, braver, stronger and braver helped me make friends with fear, period. And, I became a happy mover aging with confidence from my adventures in the parkour world. That sounds right to me. ‘Cause there’s beautiful language in here, but your audience isn’t going to do the work to make it read that way, they’re gonna kinda jump through it. And I think this paragraph is too powerful. I think you need to separate it out. I don’t know, that’s how I would write that sentence. I can’t help but edit.

[André] Yeah, I think that’s great. In fact, while we’re on this now, just really quickly, is if anybody’s interested about really unpacking what Shawn’s just said about sentences being lyrical, and word choice being important, and the value of reading things out loud, as opposed to just reading it in your head, get a book called Steering the Craft, by Ursula Le Guin, I think. I think that’s her name. She’s she’s passed on now, but it’s an amazing, amazing read. It’s very easy. But chapter one is all about this. You could probably go to Kindle and just do the free chapter thing, and you’ll probably get the whole of chapter one for free. And it just unpacks this whole idea about writing to hear the beauty of the words in a lyrical pattern. And there’s a few examples, and worth worth doing, if that interests you.

[Shawn] I’m just psyched I never heard of that book, so I’m really excited.

[André] Oh, it’s a great book.

[Shawn] Yeah, the PS, here too, a couple of opportunities for the PS, this is general observation stuff. Like this PS, if you wanna look at those early films, like at this point in time, and we’ve been yammering about it for a while, so maybe we’re throwing ourselves off. I don’t remember what the early films are. I have some vague recollection she talked about going to Southern California doing skateboard stuff. Like I don’t know how looking at those early films is in service to the broader soap opera series. So maybe seeing people move really well is in service to that. But I would probably do something, it’s probably a little early to ask for a reply, but one way you can do it, if you wanna look at those early films, reply to this email, let me know, and I’ll send you a link. You could also do PS, you know, looking back at, something basically like, mentioning the films, and saying, you might share links later. If it’s something you’re like, looking back on those films, I’m a little hesitant to share those, ’cause they’re kind of old or whatever, but I may do that on a future email. Just as a little open loop, like, oh, okay, just a little something to get my attention forward. Or no PS at all. But I don’t think this PS, I don’t know, I like a PS in the first email in general. I don’t know that I’ve ever thought about this explicitly. Just because I wanna establish that that’s likely to happen. Like look to the PS, stuff’s gonna happen here. And I like a PS, and you can do a lot of things with a PS, you can amplify the tension, you can just create a previous cliffhanger. You can have a parallel set of tension that further amplifies it in a different way. There’s so many different ways, I don’t wanna go too far, we’ll keep moving on. We made it through one email so far. Perfect. Multiple whatever we’ve spent so far times 26, and we’re good. Okay. This is act two. The want and the need, what are they looking for? Taken somewhere new, place or circumstances. They have to figure out what’s happening. Hopeless to hopeful. Oh, this is a great example of a micro theme within an email. I love, love, love, love this. So hopeless to hopeful. If you believe your audience has been burned trying something before, if something feels overwhelming from the outside, but when you break it down for them, it’s something that anybody can do, once they understand how it works, like oh, hopeless to hopeful is such a beautiful theme. Because there are two hurdles we have to overcome to get somebody to take action, to buy whatever it is. The first hurdle we have to overcome, is they have to believe that what we’re saying is true and that we can deliver on the promise that we’re making. That’s the first hurdle. That’s the one that it’s easy to cover, that’s why we talk about what’s in courses, that’s the easier one. The harder one that’s often unspoken is we have to overcome the prospect’s belief that yes, we could do it, but they can’t. There’s something wrong with them. I mean, that’s a very common problem in I would say every market. That a lot of people, and we see the feedback around, people think that the system doesn’t work, or the course isn’t right, or whatever else. But the real fear for most people isn’t that you can’t do it, you the course creator, you the product creator, it’s that they can’t do it. So hopeless to hopeful is a beautiful theme. It can be a theme that guides an entire soap opera series. And not to get too weird here, but one thing to think about themes is themes can be fractal. And fractal just means that the thing that you’re talking about repeats within itself. So a fractal theme would be, the entire theme of this soap opera series could be hopeless to hopeful. And then it could repeat itself specifically within an email, within each email. So it doesn’t have to, but it can be that way. So this is a long, long circuitous introduction to how much I love this particular theme. And I love that it’s identified. Because it’s something that Julie can refer back to, and just kind of check herself, check the writing against this. Did I do the work? Did I establish in the beginning, through dialogue, through writing, through story, through examples, hopeless? Does the audience know what hopeless feels like? And have I, by the end, given them reason, even if they’re subtle, and just the intro, have I given them a spark to be hopeful? Yes or no? Have I done the work? All right, off to you.

[André] I like the contrast that was set up early on here with the mention of Charlie’s Angels. So it was her favorite thing she used to watch. Come back on a Tuesday evening after Brownies. Do you guys get Brownies over there? Is it the same name?

– We do, yup.

[André] Okay. So she comes back from Brownies and watches the Charlie’s Angels, they’re normal chicks, but they’re not superheroes. But then at the same time, she writes, where is it? I procrastinated for six months before taking my first parkour session. So even though she was using them as this ideal, there’s these women, they’re so powerful and good, even though they aren’t superheroes, yet, she couldn’t do that. It took her six months before she went for her first lesson. So I think people reading this can relate to that, I would’ve thought.

[Shawn] Yeah. What do I think about this one? Don’t talk about parkour, is my first thought. Way too soon. ‘Cause if I’ve ever heard about parkour, and I think about the people jumping off buildings, if I have any thought about it at all, and I’ve said no to the idea in the past, it’s way too soon to be presenting me with solutions. This is way empathy. We’re so early in the empathy stage here. And this is connection, right? I don’t know which one was my favorite, but on Tuesday evening after Brownies, is I would run home to be on time to watch Charlie’s Angels. That sentence is a wonky, ’cause now I’m confused. Am I trying to figure out if Brownies or Charlie’s Angels was your favorite, or the individual Charlie’s Angels? I have no idea what I’m supposed to know. So that’s a wonky sentence. And the, I don’t know which was my favorite part, it doesn’t improve the message at all, it just muddies the message. The sentence needs to be about that you rushed home on Tuesday evenings after Brownies to watch Charlie’s Angels. That’s the sentence. Because now I can see you doing that. And then you need to tell me why. First of all, you’re telling me what you did. Rushed home on Tuesdays. And you could tell when you were a kid. You know, when I was a little girl, I’d rush home after Brownies on Tuesdays to be on time to watch Charlie’s Angels. And maybe you tell me something you didn’t do that would be a lot of fun to get home in time. And I don’t know if this is true and only write it if it’s true, but if you rushed home on Tuesday evenings after Brownies, and skipped getting a milkshake with your dad to watch Charlie’s Angels, now I know Charlie’s Angels was really important to you. And again, rewrite that part, and make it powerful. Hit me with a hammer with this first thing, so you have my attention. And so you’ve told me what, now you need to tell me why. They were fashionable, they were clever, figuring out clever in the sentence, don’t know. They were fashionable, clever detectives, something else, again, think of the lyricism here. They were this, this and this, but, they had no superpowers. That’s a juxtaposition. It’s like, wait a minute, they were… And fashionable, so this, again, we’re getting into the weeds here, but these things don’t go together. Superpowers don’t have anything to do with being fashionable. Being clever is not a superpower. So to do this right, I think, you need to talk about, what did Charlie’s Angels do that seemed like it required superpowers to do, but they didn’t have any superpowers? Well, wait a minute. To me, that’s interesting. That pulls my attention. They did all of these things that implicit would require superpowers, but they didn’t have superpowers. Well, what the hell did they have then? That’s the question I wanna know. You’ve now made me curious. And again, you’re distracting me here. Non-super hero everyday female action heroes were a scarcity by the time I started training. First of all, too soon. I don’t wanna know about your training. This is not in service to what you’re really trying to tell me here, what you’re really trying to tell me is that when you were a little girl, you would rush home on Tuesdays at the expense of other things that were fun, after doing something fun, to watch this show. And this show was so impactful for you because these were women, essentially women action heroes, female action heroes, who did these amazing things, but they didn’t have superpowers. How the hell did they do that? That’s the setup. And again, we can’t be talking about parkour yet. It’s too easy for me to check out, at this point. You need to build the tension around this idea that there was this thing that was really compelling to you, and you had to figure out how they did it. Put me in the room, when you were over your journey. However long it took for you to figure out how this group of women, who you saw on TV, who inspired you, were able to act like superheroes, do the things that superheroes do, yet they didn’t have any superpowers. How the hell is that possible? And then tell me about your journey a little bit. Like how did you figure that out? What were the first clues? Because the moment you tell me that you already figured it out, there’s no tension anymore. You said I procrastinated for six months before taking my first parkour session. Perfect. Soap opera series is over. If I wanna be cool like Charlie’s Angels, don’t wait six months, go find a parkour session, done. The tension’s gone. You’ve gotta think about that. Keep building that tension. Say something nice, ’cause I feel like I’m being a jackass.

[André] Yeah, I mean, that’s a good point. What would you write instead of parkour? Just like a training session?

[Shawn] I wouldn’t even talk about that at all yet. ‘Cause was when she gets a solution, it’s over. I wanna know how Julie felt, right? I mean, we already know in email one, let’s think about the clues she’s given us here, right? In email one, she wasn’t Mr. Blobby, your buddy there. This is how it started, right? She was too lazy and unmotivated to join a club, she wasn’t doing anything athletic. She didn’t have a home gym. She didn’t go to a gym. She felt kinda average. She wasn’t, you know, soft and squishy, she wasn’t lean and mean, either. Okay, gotcha. So if you think about it, she’s told us kinda where she was, this point, and now we’re starting to learn about how she got there. I want more journey how she got there. How did she get to that place where she was not soft and squishy, and she wasn’t lean and mean? And in order for me to understand Julie, and to connect with her, she needs to take me back to when she was a kid. And when she was a kid, her favorite show was Charlie’s Angels. And it was her favorite show because there were these women who were doing things that seemed like they had superpowers, but they didn’t. And, how the hell does that happen? Right? And that was sort of the first early sign. And then I need to know more, what were some other things that happened? Don’t tell me you figured it out. Tell me how you figured it out. And save the what you figured out for whenever it’s time for me to buy the thing that you figured out. Or reveal it later. Again, in service to this tension, we need much more. I’m just looking through this. We need much more about Julie’s journey from young girl, loving Charlie’s Angels, making this astute observation that they were like superheroes with no superpowers, to being a grown woman, and connecting how she was going to become stronger, and more capable, and to age gracefully, all of those things, it wasn’t like, something happened when she was seven, and then she figured it out when she was in her 30s, or however old she was. It’s too quick. I wanna know, what were the signs along the way? What were the two or three high points? She remembers Charlie’s Angels, what happened when she was in high school, whatever you call high school on that side of the Atlantic? Primaries, whatever, I don’t know what the hell you call it. What happened when she went, we would say college, you would say when she went to university, assuming she went to university. Or no, you call it something else in the UK, what the hell do you call it?

[André] I never went to school in the UK.

[Shawn] That’s true, that’s right. But anyway. So European, I can’t think of the term. Anyway, you get it. Along the way, there were probably, I don’t know, three inflection points that Julie could look back on, that with the benefit of hindsight, she could probably see that three or four times, she was starting to see this thing that mattered. She saw it when she was a young girl embodied in Charlie’s Angels. I’m sure she saw it later a few times. She certainly saw it with the adventure athletes that she was filming in Southern California. And if you’ve ever seen anyone skating in a pool, you know the courage that takes, and the athleticism that takes. So, if I were writing this whole series, I would actually write a timeline. You can’t see my hands, because I don’t want you to see me wearing this dress, so I don’t have the camera on. But I have my hands from left to right. And what I would do is I would have Julie’s three inflection points. Act one, set the stage. Act two, Julie’s journey. When did she see glimpses of this particular type of athleticism? And then act three, how did Julie put things together, and how did she transform her life when she found parkour, without naming it? Or name it. She found this thing, explain what it is. Talk about it a little bit in act three. And then how can the audience get the same benefit that Julie got, that took her a lot longer? That to me is the timeline. And again, the only reason I can do the timeline is because Julie’s done the work to get the thoughts out of her head. The thing that I’m doing right now is so ridiculously easy, because she’s already done the work. If she hadn’t done the work, the editing, you can’t edit something that’s not written. So you have to do this work that Julie has done to get all of these thoughts out of your head, and then you can start sequencing them in a way that builds and releases tension. But if you don’t do the work, you can’t do that. It’s really hard to do a review of a blank page. They’re short, but there’s not much that you can do. So I just wanna reinforce that Julie has done the important first step here, so I hope nobody takes any of this as criticism. It’s not. It’s editing. And editing’s just part of the process. That’s all. All right, I’m handing it back to you, ’cause I’m tired of hearing my own voice.

[André] Yeah, and I think that’s a good point you’ve drawn attention to, the fact that, don’t beat yourself up about your shitty first draft. I do a draft zero. It’s just to get the stuff out my head. It’s definitely not an order. ‘Cause my thoughts are never in order. So I need to get them out onto a page, which is my draft zero. Only after that does the editing start to take shape. So we’re looking at this now, and as Shawn said, there’s words on the page, so we can kind of move this around a bit. I like the idea of getting people to come to the conclusion that this is parkour before she says it explicitly. So even if it’s in the same email, ’cause at some point inside here, she is talking about running around some London estate places. But I think, well, she certainly mentions parkour before that. So she’s queued it up. Someone’s thinking about this. But if the narrative is just taking somebody into this place, and she’s watching these people jumping around, that might be more interesting, because then people are thinking, isn’t that called parkour? And then, you know, later, she says, yeah, and then I found out that it’s called this thing called parkour. Yeah, I’m just thinking out loud. I think that kind of maybe works better.

[Shawn] Yeah, I mean, that’s the squirt of dopamine. Apparently every recording has to reference an oscilloscope and a squirt of dopamine before it’s complete, so now we’re done. But that’s the reveal, right? We want the audience, we’re giving them two plus two. That’s the unifying theory of two plus two from the TED video, that I think we have linked somewhere in there.

[André] Yes we do.

[Shawn] So, The Keys to a Good Story, or whatever. But when we give the audience four, they get less value out of it. And when we give them four right in the beginning, I mean, imagine the difference in suspense if we spent three emails establishing two, and then the next three emails establishing plus two, and then, at that next point, if we just give them four, we’ve relieved the tension. But if we give them two plus two, and then we give them another three emails where we’re giving them clues that the two and the two equals something, and then they realize, oh, shit, it’s parkour. They figure it out. They get that emotional a-ha moment. Now contrast this with what happens in this soap opera series, which is we give them four right in the beginning. We’re like, hey, it’s four. And then we start giving them two. And then we tell them again, as we’re giving them two, whoops, that it’s four. And then we give them a couple more things to establish the next two. But we keep reminding them that it’s four, that by the time we get to email six or whatever, there’s nothing to reveal. We’ve already told them the whole thing. We’ve solved the equation for them. There’s nothing for them to do. So from a conceptual standpoint, this series really wants to be three emails establishing the first part, which is, you can imagine that as the initial two. Like, what’s the background? Or maybe the first two is, here’s Julie at this inflection point. That’s what we’re establishing first. She wasn’t squishy and soft, This is weird, talking about another person like this, especially a person I don’t know personally. So she wasn’t squishy and soft, and she wasn’t lean and mean, right? I can’t help but speak lyrically. So, that’s the first two. So now I can imagine Julie, like, okay, yeah, we’ve all been in that place where we’re like, you know what? I’m not at my worst, but I’m not at my best either. I’m just in that middle space. So we can relate to that. That’s the first thing we want the audience to connect with. I think that’s the first email all itself. I think it’s really digging deep into, what was that, how did she get there? But then, the next thing we want to contrast that with, the next part of the equation is, Julie has seen, in her past, a version of what lean and mean is. Her first memory of that is Charlie’s Angels. They were like superheroes with no superpowers. So there was something there. So the next few emails are, it’s the second part of two plus two. The first part is Julie, where she is, how she feels. Second part is the vision of what she knows is possible that she’s seen over the course of her life. Then, don’t rush and give me four, and tell me it’s parkour that got you there. Begin to take me on the journey that led you to parkour. You can even describe what you did, just don’t ever use the word. For people who don’t know, parkour is also called free running. Parkour is a French term. But I believe it translates into free running. I don’t know if it’s a direct translation, but if you’ve seen parkour, it’s this very dynamic, outside, very natural type of movement. So Julie can describe the actual movement. She can describe reading the book Born to Run, and running barefoot, and be interested in that. I read Born to Run, I had a very similar experience. But she can describe all of those things, but never say parkour. Because, if she doesn’t, that’s the four that the audience has to fill in for themselves. ‘Cause if they can actually see Julie running barefoot out in a park, at some point, someone who is familiar with it is going to go, that sounds like parkour, right? That’s when they’re like, a-ha, I figured out what she’s talking about. And that feels good, as the reader. And then at some point, she can bring it all together. She can name it, in passing. And mention that this has a name, and this is what it’s about, and this it’s so valuable, and this is what it does for people who do it. But now, when she reveals it way later, she has captured my attention so thoroughly, and that’s the business we’re in. We’re in the business of sustained attention. And the way to sustained attention is to have the thing that’s going to eventually resolve the tension far enough in the future that I’m buying in for the whole journey. That’s what I feel like is really missing here, is all of the parts are here. All of the writing is here. It’s just not in the right order. And it’s revealed too soon, I think. That’s my gut assessment of this one.

[André] Yeah, definitely, I agree. By the time you mention parkour, they should be, yeah, I knew that already. And you have their buy-in at that point. Now they’re not mentally opting out, having a frame of reference about something that they maybe don’t know enough about, and they’ve just heard it on the news that it’s really dangerous, so it’s like, ah, yeah, I’m not gonna do that. But by the time they’re way deeper into this narrative, and you’ve got them there in this way that’s very engaging, especially when you figured out, from going on this journey, a few things, where you’re just using parkour as a mechanism to do movement. I don’t know the course, but maybe you’re using parkour in a different way. But by the time you mention it, people are gonna be on board about that, I think. At least the ones that need to be.

[Shawn] Yeah, there’s just so much here. I feel like at this point, I don’t know. I think we’re just gonna start repeating ourselves. But I think what you said earlier, what you just reiterated there is so much of the magic that’s easy to forget, which is, let people have a sense of discovery. That’s so important. I forget that. It’s certainly if I have an Achilles heel, it’s that. It’s, I want to get to the thing, and talk about the thing, because the thing is so interesting to me. And I think not talking about the thing, but talking about how you got there, and this is where I struggle so much, ’cause I’m so precise in my writing, that it feels so uncomfortable to me to be talking about something and not explicitly name it, ’cause I don’t think that way. But it’s such an important insight for our audience to hear, is that that’s really the job that we’re doing. Is we’re setting things up so that eventually, the person has that sense of discovery. It’s such a good word. That they feel like when you say it, there’s almost a sense of like, I knew it. I knew that’s what you were talking about. Because that feels good, you feel like a hero. And what a way to make your audience feel excellent. Is to make them feel smart. To engineer a sequence of emails, a series of emails, where the payoff is for them to feel smart before you reveal something to them. That’s brilliant on so many levels. I’m fixated on something here, so I wanna I focus on this. This is my own mania, so take this with a grain of salt, but here’s this paragraph, right? So, I also had a serendipitous encounter with my old Tai Chi teacher on Ladbroke Grove, introduced me to Rolfing. Rolfing, yeah, I did Rolfing, so I should know how to pronounce it. Fascia manipulation. As he fixed three years of bilateral Achilles tendonitis, a 30 minute sweaty-palmed Chinese burns type session, that made me swear at him a lot, like good friends do. This is a really interesting paragraph. I have no idea if it adds any value whatsoever. And I feel like I’ve had to work so hard to get through that, that I would just be cautious of these kinds of paragraphs. It’s one of those things like, it’s really easy to add something that feels like we’re kinda adding some color. And if you’re going to add this, you need to make it a lot more succinct. You know, this is a lot, and maybe this is just how you write naturally, and this is your first draft, but there’s a lot here. You know, this could be a sentence. You know, I also had a serendipitous encounter with my old Tai Chi teacher who fixed three years of ankle problems in 30 minutes, and it hurt like, hell, right? Make it easy to understand. ‘Cause I don’t know what half of this means. I don’t know what Achilles tendonitis is, I kinda have an idea. If you don’t know what Rolfing is, then that’s a challenge too. And worse, if you don’t know what fascia is, now, you’re doubly confused. There’s just so much here to derail things. Say things in a way that you wish that you could just say to somebody very succinctly in a conversation. ‘Cause if Julie and I ran into each other, right after this happened, she wouldn’t say it to me this way. She’d be like, oh, you’re never gonna believe this, I just ran into my old Tai Chi teacher. And, this is how this conversation would happen between two friends. And treat your audience like friends. So if I ran into Julie on the street, first of all, she would not say serendipitous, right? People don’t talk like that. You might say something that means serendipitous, but you probably wouldn’t use the word. But she would probably say like, you’re never gonna believe this, I just ran into my old Tai Chi teacher. My ankle’s been killing me for three years, and he did this weird thing where he like, this weird, Chinese mysticism, did this thing. 30 minutes later, it feels fine. That’s so weird, right? That’s how two people would talk to each other about something like this. So just go back to the, and I screw this up all the time too, so this is me talking to myself. But just remember when you’re sharing something with somebody, think about how you would tell a friend. And at least get that out of your head onto the page, and then kind of mess with it from there. All right, I have this feeling that anything we say beyond much longer, we’re going to start making things worse, so-

[André] Yeah, so let me just say-

[Shawn] Yeah, let’s wrap things up. I’ll say one tiny thing at the end, but you take us home here, ’cause I think I’m tired of hearing my own voice.

[André] So that sentence that you’ve just unpacked, with the Rolfing, when I’m writing my draft zeros, and these stories that seem interesting show up on the page, I don’t judge them in the moment, I just get them out. And many times, they’ll come out like this, where there’s just too much details that are irrelevant. And you know, I’ve always found it very difficult to kill my babies. What’s the saying? Something like that.

[Shawn] Kill your darlings, right?

[André] Darlings, that’s it. And, one of the exercises in the book that I cited early on, because at the end of every chapter, there’s a few writing exercises, if you wanna do the exercises, and one of the ones she mentions is, you should be able to read almost any book, take any story, and the exercise is make it half as long. So cut it in half, and retain all the meaning. And, it’s a difficult thing to do, but it can certainly be done. And in many cases, the story is better for it. So, yeah. So just below that-

[Shawn] That’s my job, is to kill your darlings. It’s so funny. We never talk about this, but I always feel bad. I mean, I feel bad right up until I don’t, but from our writing process, you couldn’t have two different writers. From a process perspective.

[André] I’m always giving Shawn darlings.

[Shawn] Often, when André does the first, what I consider the base writing. One of us usually writes the base, which is just the thing, and then we collaborate on it. But, there are just so many times I get handed something, and I feel bad for a moment, but it’s like, yeah, half of this just disappears in the first five minutes, it’s gone. And it’s not because the writing’s bad, it just becomes, what’s the main thing that we’re talking about, and what is necessary, what is required in service to that main point, and what is it? And then you just take it away. And I can’t remember where the quote’s from, I think it was from the author of The Little Prince, it’s one of my favorite quotes, and I can’t pronounce his name, ’cause his name is French, so I never do. But it’s this idea that beauty is not when there’s nothing left to add, it’s when there’s nothing left to take away. And it’s such a different distinction because we think, I mean, if you think about Michelangelo’s David, the statue, there was a block of stone, and inside, and apparently this quote is not accurate, but there was this idea that Michelangelo removed everything that wasn’t David, and what remained was the statute. But that’s beauty, right? The things that are not David have been removed. So a lot of editing is not, you’re taking away the things that are not required for the beauty that’s there to reveal itself in a very powerful way. Now, you can choose to add more. And oftentimes, there’s a great reason to do that, in that you can elaborate on things, but there’s a real elegant beauty, certainly influenced by my background as a journalist, to put that in perspective, but there’s a real beauty to be able to remove 50, 60, 70% of a piece of writing, and just see what remains. Now that doesn’t mean that’s the final, but it gives you a structure by which oftentimes, the final can be built. So, Julie doesn’t have to remove this paragraph. But it’s an opportunity to ask herself two questions. One, does this belong, in general, does this further the narrative that I’ve created for my audience, in a meaningful way? And if the answer to that is no, remove it. If the answer to that is, yes, the next question is, how can I make this better? And this can be made better by making it one sentence. Removing a lot of all the jargon and the details. This can just be one sentence that says the same thing. So that was a fun tangent. Yikes. Thanks for setting that one up. Sorry I killed some of your darlings.

[André] That’s why I send them to you. ‘Cause I can’t kill them.

[Shawn] All right, just to be fair, though. I usually move your darlings. I don’t kill them all. Just so the audience doesn’t feel like, why are you working with that jackass? To be clear for everybody who’s listening, I tend to structure André’s darlings. Sometimes I put them in different places, but very rarely do any get killed. We don’t kill darlings around here. All right. Say something smart to wrap this up, so we can hand this off to Julie.

[André] Yeah, on that bombshell. She’s got so many nice phrases and words here that I can’t wait to read this once it’s been redone again. This visual idea of not running in straight lines. What’s she got here? Micro bravery. You can think about stuff like that. It feels attainable. This micro bravery, that idea, and not running in straight lines. There’s so much here that I think can be strong when it’s wrapped in a narrative that’s a lot more cleaner when you get there. So, I mean, we’re only on page, what? Page two or three now, of many. So Julie’s got lots of work to do. And I think this has been broadly helpful for everybody. Even though there’s is a different story, I think most of the things we’ve spoken about, we all face every single time we stare at a blank page, and start to spew out something. Especially when we’ve done our draft zero, our draft one, and it’s taking shape, but it’s still far from what needs to happen before it can be stuck in front of somebody. So, yeah, I think that’s what I’ll end on. I think this is really good writing.

– Yeah, this is beautiful. There’s so much work that went into this. And I don’t know how Julie did this, but I’m imagining that she put her butt in a chair, and wrote 26 pages. And that is an act of macro bravery. And an act of conviction. That is an act of sustained bravery and conviction. And that’s the work. We can say this a million times in a million different ways, but it all comes down to the simple thing that you have to do the work. And there’s no way around that. So there are different ways to do the work. I wouldn’t have written this series the way that Julie did. But the way I would have written it, isn’t better, it isn’t worse, it’s just unique to how I write. The fact that she sat down and did this is profound, absolutely profound. Because now, she’s in the easy stage. And the easy stage is just sequencing thoughts and putting them in a way that’s coherent. It’s part of the work. But you can’t sequence something that doesn’t exist. You can’t structure something that doesn’t exist. So what she’s done here is, I mean, I couldn’t be more impressed. At the macro level, it’s 26 pages of content to arrange into a soap opera series. At the micro level, there are these beautiful vignettes throughout the entirety of this, and they could inspire new series, they could be subtext within the larger, there’s so much to work with here. And at the nano level, there are just these beautiful sentences, and beautiful expressions of thought, there’s just so much here. So this was a real privilege. Everything is. Reviewing anybody’s work is a privilege. But this was just so much fun, to see somebody take the work so seriously, and to produce something of this magnitude, is just profound. So I’ll leave that with a thank you to Julie, and thank you for giving us this opportunity to read this work, because this was a real privilege. All right, let’s wrap it up for the day. Thank you, everybody.
